title: Sugar flowers
subtitle: Cake decoration technique for making edible flowers from rolled fondant
excerpt: Hand-shaped edible flowers made from rolled fondant stiffened with a little cornflour. Covers simple five-petal blossoms pinched from a ball of paste, spiral roses built from overlapping petals, and the basic skills of colouring, shaping, and drying. No specialist tools required for the basic forms. Flowers must dry completely before use so they hold their shape when placed on a cake.
sourceNotes: Sugar flower work is a traditional element of wedding and celebration cake decoration. The techniques for modelling flowers from sugar paste and rolled fondant are documented in British cake decorating literature from the nineteenth century. Sugar paste flower modelling as a domestic skill is described in British confectionery and decorating guides consistent with the modern approach, including the use of stiffened paste, hand shaping, and air drying before placement.

content.text: The foundation of all sugar flower work is
getting the paste to the right consistency
: firm enough to hold a shape, soft enough not to crack when shaped thin. Work small amounts at a time; paste dries out quickly once exposed to air.

attrs.prepNote: for kneading into paste and dusting the work surface
gel paste colours give more vivid results than liquid; use as little as possible and knead until even

content.text: Knead the rolled fondant with the cornflour until smooth and slightly firmer than it started. To colour, add a tiny amount of gel food colouring and knead until the colour is completely even; use gloves to avoid staining your hands.

Simple five-petal blossom: roll a small ball of paste (about 5g) on a surface lightly dusted with cornflour until smooth. Flatten it with your thumb to a rough disc about 3cm across. Pinch five petals around the edge by pressing the paste between your thumb and forefinger and pulling gently outward while rotating. Use the tip of a knife or skewer to indent a centre. Place on baking paper to dry.

Simple spiral rose: roll a strip of paste about 20cm long and 2cm wide, slightly thicker at one edge and thinner at the other. Starting from one end, roll it up loosely into a spiral, keeping the thicker base downward. Pinch the base firmly to hold the shape. Separate the outer petals gently with your fingers to open the rose. Place on baking paper to dry.

Leave all flowers to dry uncovered at room temperature for at least 24 hours before placing on a cake. They should feel completely firm and dry to the touch. Attach to the cake using a small amount of water or edible glue brushed onto the base of each flower.

recipe.yieldDescription: varies; about 12 to 20 small flowers from 250g paste
